content.full_text:
April 5, 1994


Darrel W. Larsen
Office of the County Counsel
County of Sutter
1160 Civic Center Boulevard
Yuba City, California 95993

Re:  Your Request for Advice
     Our File No. A-94-048

Dear Mr. Larsen:

     This is in response to your request for advice on behalf of
Sutter County Board of Supervisors Dick Akin and Casey Kroon
regarding the conflict-of-interest provisions of the Political
Reform Act (the "Act").

     Please note that nothing in this letter should be construed
to evaluate any conduct which may have already taken place. In
addition, this letter is based on the facts presented to us. The
Commission does not act as the finder of fact in providing advice.
(In re Oglesby (1975) 1 FPPC Ops. 71.)

QUESTION

     May Sutter County Supervisors Akin and Kroon participate in
the consideration of a proposed amendment to a county zoning code
to require a use permit for storage of flammable liquids and
liquified petroleum gases on property zoned General Agricultural,
including the property in which they have an interest?

CONCLUSION

     Supervisors Akin and Kroon may not participate in the
consideration of the proposed amendment to the zoning code, unless


1     Government Code Sections 81000-91015. All statutory references
are to the Government Code unless otherwise indicated. Commission
regulations appear at 2 California Code of Regulations Sections,
18000 - 18954. All references to regulations are to Title 2,
Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations.


428 J Street, Suite 800 • P.O. Box 807 • Sacramento, CA  95804-0807 • (916) 322-5660

Our File No. A-94-048
Page 2


a significant segment of the public generally is affected in a
similar manner, because the imposition of a fee on the officials'
property in conjunction with a use permit will have a foreseeable
and material financial effect on their property.

FACTS

The Sutter County Board of Supervisors is considering a
proposed amendment to a zoning ordinance to authorize the storage
of flammable liquids and liquified petroleum gases in a portion of
the county zoned General agricultural (AG), subject to obtaining a
use permit. The fee proposed for the use permit is $300.

Supervisors Akin and Kroon, both elected by district
elections, own property in the AG-zoned area. Supervisor Akin
owns a farm, and has two stationary fuel tanks at his home as well
as a number of portable tanks. Supervisor Kroon also owns a farm
and has both a fuel tank and a propane tank on the property.

You stated during our telephone conversation of March 25,
1994, that there are 7,611 property owners and 850 farms in
Supervisor Akin's district and 6,003 property owners and 650 farms
in Supervisor Kroon's district. You previously stated in another
telephone conversation on March 7, 1994, that it is estimated that
approximately 30 percent of the farms in the county will be
affected by the imposition of a conditional use permit.

We have calculated, on the basis of your facts, that 255
property owners within Supervisor Akin's district (30 percent of
850 farms within the district) and 195 property owners within
Supervisor Kroon's district (30 percent of 650 farms within the
district) would be affected by the decision. Therefore,
approximately 3.4 percent (255) of the 7,611 property owners
within Supervisor Akin's district and 3.2 percent (195) of the
6,003 property owners within Supervisor Kroon's district would be
affected by the decision.2

ANALYSIS

Section 87100 prohibits any public official from making,
participating in making, or otherwise using his or her official
position to influence a governmental decision in which the
official has a financial interest. As members of the Sutter
County Board of Supervisors, Supervisors Akin and Kroon are public
officials under the Act. (Section 82048.)


2 This assumes that the number of farms is proportionately
representative of the number of property owners within each
member's district.

Our File No. A-94-048
Page 3


Economic Interests

     Section 87103 specifies that a public official has a
financial interest in a decision if it is reasonably foreseeable
that the decision will have a material financial effect,
distinguishable from the effect on the public generally, on the
official or a member of his or her immediate family or on any real
property in which the public official has a direct or indirect
interest worth $1,000 or more. (Section 87103(b).) Presumably,
Supervisors Akin and Kron have an interest in real property in an
amount of at least $1,000.

     Thus, these officials may not participate in any decision
that will have a foreseeable and material financial effect on
their real property. Whether the financial consequence of a
decision are reasonably foreseeable at the time a governmental
decision is made depends on the facts of each particular case. An
effect is considered reasonably foreseeable if there is a
substantial likelihood that it will occur. Certainty is not
required. However, if an effect is only a mere possibility, it is
not reasonably foreseeable. (In re Thorner (1975) 1 FPPC Ops.
198.)

     You have indicated that a use permit authorizing the storage
of flammable liquids and liquefied petroleum gases would be
required in areas of the county zoned AG. The amount charged for
the permit would be $300. Thus, it is foreseeable that decisions
to amend the county's zoning ordinance will have a financial
effect on the officials' real property interests.

     Moreover, where a governmental decision involves the
imposition, repeal or modification of any taxes or fees assessed
or imposed on an official's property, Regulation 18702.1(a)(3)(C)
provides that the effect is deemed material and the official is
required to disqualify himself or herself from the decision.³ The
decision involves the imposition of fees on the officials'
property by requiring a use permit in an amount of $300.
Therefore, the officials will be materially affected by the
decisions and they may not participate in the decisions.

The "Public Generally" Exception

     Even when the reasonably foreseeable effect of a governmental
decision is material, disqualification is required only if the

     ³ Please note that Regulation 18702.1(a)(3)(E) applies to
decisions involving the establishment or changing of the zoning or
land use designation of an official's property. Based on your
facts, the decision involves an imposition of a fee on the
official's property. Therefore, Regulation 18702.1(a)(3)(E) does
not control.

Our File No. A-94-048
Page 4


effect of the decision on an official's economic interest is
distinguishable from the effect on the public generally. (Section
87103; Regulation 18703 (enclosed).) For the "publicly
generally" exception to apply, any decision would have to affect a
significant segment of the population of the jurisdiction, or
district an official represents, in substantially the same manner
as it would affect a disqualified public official.

A governmental decision will affect a significant segment of
the public generally if any of the following apply:

     1.  The decision will affect ten percent or more of all
property owners, all home owners, or all households in the
jurisdiction of the official's agency or the district the official
represents. (Regulation 18703(a)(1)(A)(i).)

     2.  The decision will affect 50 percent of all businesses in
the jurisdiction or the district the official represents, so long
as the segment is composed of persons other than a single
industry, trade, or profession. (Regulation 1870(a)(1)(A)(ii).)

     3.    The decision will affect 5,000 individuals who are
residents of the jurisdiction. (Regulation 18703(a)(1)(B).)

     For the "public generally" exception to apply to this
situation, the zoning decisions must affect a significant segment
of all property owners within the county, or within each member's
district, in substantially the same manner as it would affect each
official's real property. Therefore, under Regulation
18703(a)(1)(A)(i), the decision must affect ten percent of all
property owners in the jurisdiction, or the district the official
represents, not ten percent of all the farms within the
jurisdiction or within each member's district.

     On the basis of your facts, it appears that only 3.4 percent
(255) of property owners within Supervisor Akin's district and 3.2
percent (195) within Supervisor Kroon's district would be affected
by the decision. Therefore, it does not meet that the "public
generally" exception under Regulation 18703(a)(1)(A)(i) would
apply.  However, it is also possible that the exception would
apply if 5,000 individuals who are residents of the jurisdiction
would be affected in the same manner by the decision. (Regulation
18703 (a)(1)(B).) Therefore, if this exception applies to your
facts,⁵ the officials may participate in the decision.

___________________________
     4  In your letter you indicated that there were 2,700 farms in the
district. Under the "publicly generally" regulation, the segment
must represent a significant segment of all property owners, not
of the total number of farms within the jurisdiction or district.

     ⁵  As noted earlier, the Commission does not act as the finder of
fact in providing advice. (In re Oglesby, supra.)

Our File No. A-94-048
Page 5


If you have any further questions regarding this matter,
please feel free to contact me at (916) 322-5901.6

                                        Sincerely,

                                        Steven G. Churchwell
                                        General Counsel

                                        [signature]
                                        By: Luisa Menchaca
                                            Counsel, Legal Division

Enclosure




6 Copies of Commission regulations and Opinions are available in
many law libraries. Alternatively, copies of these materials and
Commission advice letters may be obtained from the Commission at a
cost of 10¢ per page.
